ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken karaage rice bowl; 'rice bowl dish' (FOODON_00002809) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, fried, with rice — composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated karaage don code; A0CKP (fried chicken with rice composite) is the closest defensible analogue. Documented as closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken karaage rice bowl; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken karaage rice bowl weighing approximately 450 g, comprising deep-fried marinated chicken pieces served over steamed white rice with optional garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken thigh, fried, battered or breaded (FDC ID 171477) and White rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878), combined as composite dish estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for chicken karaage rice bowl as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for fried battered chicken thigh (FDC ID 171477) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), weighted approximately 40% chicken and 60% rice by mass to reflect a typical karaage don composition. Input per_100g values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA composite estimate and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived composite is used as the primary evidence basis. Sodium value (266.7 mg/100g) is plausible given soy sauce marinade. All values retained from input and validated against USDA composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (cooked, Japanese-style, GI ~72) as dominant carbohydrate component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for chicken karaage rice bowl. The GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The fried chicken coating (potato starch and wheat flour) contributes additional rapidly digestible starch but represents a smaller carbohydrate fraction. A blended GI of 72 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 72 × (12.22 × 4.5 / 100) = 72 × 0.5499 = 39.6 g per 450 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 266.7 mg/100g (approximately 1200 mg per 450 g serving) approaches one-half of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in the marinade, avoid added condiments such as mayonnaise, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (estimated GI 72) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, combined with low dietary fibre (0.67 g/100g) and a refined starch coating on the fried chicken, produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, increase vegetable garnishes to add fibre, and request baked or air-fried chicken to reduce refined starch coating impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (1.78 g/100g) and trans fat (0.07 g/100g), and the high sodium content (266.7 mg/100g) is a risk factor for blood pressure elevation relevant to cardiovascular outcomes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request air-fried or baked preparation to reduce saturated and trans fat intake, and avoid high-sodium condiments to manage overall cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which directly exacerbate joint inflammation, overriding any anti-inflammatory benefit from ginger in the marinade.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request baked or air-fried preparation to eliminate AGE formation, and avoid pro-inflammatory condiments such as mayonnaise containing omega-6 oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Karaage Rice Bowl (鶏の唐揚げ丼, Tori no Karaage Don) is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish consisting of crispy deep-fried marinated chicken pieces served over a bed of steamed white rice. The chicken is typically marinated in soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and sesame oil before being coated in potato starch and wheat flour, then deep-fried until golden and crunchy on the outside while remaining juicy inside. The dish is commonly garnished with fresh vegetables such as shredded lettuce, cherry tomatoes, and a drizzle of sauce or mayonnaise. In Singapore, this dish is widely available through Japanese fast-casual restaurants and food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 38 g fat, and 55 g carbohydrate. The dish has a high glycemic index (estimated GI 72) due to white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, and moderate-to-high sodium content (approximately 1200 mg per serving) from the soy sauce marinade. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification. The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Substituting white rice with brown rice and requesting air-fried preparation can significantly improve the nutritional profile of this dish.
common_user_queries: Calories in chicken karaage rice bowl
Is chicken karaage bowl suitable for diabetics?
Japanese fried chicken rice bowl nutrition
Can I eat karaage bowl if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for chicken karaage rice bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for fried battered chicken thigh and cooked white rice, weighted by estimated component proportions. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with the USDA composite estimate and have been retained with USDA as the primary evidence basis. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white rice, GI 72) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fried chicken and rice dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; individual component entries do not provide sufficient precision for a reliable composite fatty acid profile. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not available in a single approved source entry; estimation from components was not performed to avoid compounding uncertainty. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available as a single approved source entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-17. 4. trans_fat_g (0.07 g/100g) is retained from input; small amounts of trans fat are plausible from deep-frying in vegetable oil. 5. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-specific certification; the dish may be halal depending on preparation. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for chicken karaage rice bowl. 7. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; actual GI of the composite dish may differ. 8. Input source listed foodo.sg URL but the organization was HPB Singapore; the actual nutrient evidence basis used is USDA FoodData Central as the approved source for this Japanese dish type. 9. This dish is sold in Singapore by a Japanese fast-casual restaurant chain; multilingual names for Chinese (鸡唐扬饭 / 雞唐揚飯), Malay (Mangkuk Nasi Ayam Goreng Karaage), Tamil (கோழி கராகே சாதம்), and Hindi (चिकन कराआगे राइस बाउल) have been added to satisfy G6 Singaporean market multilingual requirements given the dish is served and consumed in Singapore.
